your rep scheme matters more than your exercise selection. 3x10 and 5x5 on the same movement produce measurably different adaptations. schoenfeld (2021) showed high-rep sets (30-rep range) built equivalent hypertrophy to 8-12 reps when taken to failure.

**Generated:** 2026-02-13
**Voice:** Evidence-based training. confident but not guru. lowercase. no emojis. real citations. calls out fitness BS directly.
**Tagline:** "bro science meets real science. the data doesn't care about your feelings.

trained fasted for 30 days. tracked every session. strength dropped 4% in week 1, recovered by week 3. net difference after 30 days: zero. but i lost 2.1 lbs more fat. the research checks out. (schoenfeld et al., 2014) showed similar findings across 20 subjects. #bodybuilding...

creatine is the most studied supplement in history. 700+ peer-reviewed studies. 5g daily. no loading phase needed (hultman et al., 1996 proved saturation at 28 days either way). no cycling needed. costs $0.15/day. if you're not taking it you're leaving free gains on the table....
progressive overload is the only non-negotiable in training. not your split. not your exercise choice. not your tempo. mattocks et al. #nutrition

"you need 1g of protein per pound of bodyweight" has been repeated so many times it became gospel. the actual research ceiling is 0.73g/lb (morton et al., 2018 meta-analysis of 49 studies, 1,863 participants). a 180 lb guy needs 131g, not 180g.

tracked my sleep vs gym performance for 90 days. nights with <6 hours sleep: average rpe 8.2 on working sets, missed reps 34% of sessions. nights with 7-9 hours: rpe 7.1, missed reps 6% of sessions. sleep isn't a "recovery tool." it's the base layer. #bodybuilding #fitfam

the smith machine gets more hate than any piece of equipment in the gym and most of it is wrong. schwanbeck et al. (2020) found no significant difference in quad activation between smith squat and free barbell squat. the smith machine isn't ideal.

stretching before lifting doesn't prevent injuries. a cochrane review (lauersen et al., 2014) of 26,610 participants found static stretching had no significant effect on injury reduction. what does? proper warm-up sets with progressive loading.

cold plunges after lifting blunt the hypertrophy response. roberts et al. (2015) showed 10 min cold water immersion post-resistance training reduced muscle protein synthesis by ~25% and blunted satellite cell activity over 12 weeks.

you don't need a deload week every 4 weeks. you need one when performance drops. tracked my training for 6 months using rir logging. average time between necessary deloads: 6.3 weeks. sometimes 4, sometimes 9. autoregulation beats rigid programming every time. (zourdos et al....

every "natural transformation" account that gained 30 lbs of muscle in 12 months is lying to you or on gear. the natural ceiling for muscle gain is roughly 20-25 lbs in year 1 for males (mcdonald model, validated by helms et al., 2014). #bodybuilding
### thread 1: "5 fitness myths i believed for years. the studies that changed my mind."

rest periods matter more than you think. schoenfeld et al. (2016) compared 1 min vs 3 min rest between sets over 8 weeks. the 3-min group gained significantly more muscle and strength. both groups did the same exercises, same reps, same sets. the only variable was rest. #train...

the "anabolic window" is mostly dead. schoenfeld & aragon (2018) meta-analysis found total daily protein intake matters far more than timing. eating within 2 hours vs 6 hours post-workout made no significant difference in hypertrophy or strength when daily protein was equated....

people spend $200/month on supplements and sleep 5 hours a night. creatine works. caffeine works. protein powder is convenient food, not a supplement. everything else has weak or zero evidence for trained individuals. (kerksick et al., 2018 issn position stand).
